expanded_description: Learn to predict the three-dimensional shapes of molecules using VSEPR theory, understanding how electron pair repulsion determines molecular geometry and influences physical and chemical properties.

core_concept.explanation: VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion) theory states that electron pairs around a central atom arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion. This arrangement determines the molecular geometry, which directly affects polarity, reactivity, and physical properties.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron pair repulsion and molecular shapes
image.caption: Figure 6.5.1: VSEPR theory—electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs) repel each other, determining molecular geometry.

sections.introduction: VSEPR theory, developed in the 1950s, provides a simple yet powerful method for predicting molecular shapes based on the number of electron pairs around a central atom. The theory rests on the principle that electron pairs repel each other and will adopt arrangements that minimize this repulsion.

key_concept.definition: VSEPR theory predicts molecular geometry by counting electron domains (bonding pairs and lone pairs) around a central atom and arranging them to minimize repulsion.
key_concept.context: Electron domains include single bonds, double bonds, triple bonds (each counts as one domain), and lone pairs.

characteristics: Bonding domain: shared electron pair (single, double, or triple bond counts as 1 domain)
Nonbonding domain: lone pair on central atom
Each domain occupies space and repels other domains
classifications.behavior: Lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs because they are closer to the nucleus and occupy more space.

examples.explanation: 4 bonding domains, 0 lone pairs → tetrahedral geometry.
3 bonding domains, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry.

image.caption: Three bonding + one lone pair domain.

classifications.value: Lone Pair-Lone Pair > Lone Pair-Bonding > Bonding-Bonding
characteristics: Lone pair-lone pair repulsion is strongest
Lone pair-bonding pair repulsion is intermediate
Bonding pair-bonding pair repulsion is weakest
classifications.behavior: Stronger repulsions compress bond angles, explaining deviations from ideal geometry.

examples.explanation: Water has 2 lone pairs compressing $\text{H}-\text{O}-\text{H}$ angle to $104.5^\circ$; methane has no lone pairs, ideal $109.5^\circ$.

image.caption: Lone pairs reduce bond angles from ideal.

principles.statement: Count all electron domains around the central atom: each bond (single, double, or triple) = 1 domain; each lone pair = 1 domain.
principles.application: In $\text{CO}_2$, carbon has 2 double bonds = 2 domains → linear geometry. In $\text{SO}_2$, sulfur has 2 bonds + 1 lone pair = 3 domains → bent geometry.
principles.analogy: Like balloons tied together: each balloon (electron domain) pushes away from others, creating predictable shapes.

sections.introduction: A critical distinction in VSEPR theory: electron geometry describes the arrangement of all electron domains, while molecular geometry describes only the arrangement of atoms (ignoring lone pairs).

key_concept.definition: Electron geometry includes all electron domains (bonding + lone pairs); molecular geometry includes only the positions of atoms (bonding domains only).
key_concept.context: Lone pairs influence molecular shape but are not visible in molecular geometry names.

components.interpretation: Electron geometry is determined by total domains; molecular geometry is determined by atom positions after ignoring lone pairs.

image.alt: Comparison of electron and molecular geometry
image.caption: Figure 6.5.2: Electron geometry includes lone pairs; molecular geometry shows only atoms.

categories.description: Linear electron geometry with $180^\circ$ bond angles.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Two domains maximize separation by positioning $180^\circ$ apart.

examples.explanation: Electron geometry: linear; Molecular geometry: linear.
Electron geometry: linear; Molecular geometry: linear.

categories.description: Trigonal planar electron geometry with $120^\circ$ bond angles.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Three domains arrange in a plane, $120^\circ$ apart, minimizing repulsion.

examples.explanation: Electron geometry: trigonal planar; Molecular geometry: trigonal planar.

image.caption: Trigonal planar geometry, all atoms in one plane.

examples.explanation: Electron geometry: trigonal planar; Molecular geometry: bent ($\sim 119^\circ$).

image.caption: Lone pair creates bent molecular shape.

categories.description: Tetrahedral electron geometry with $109.5^\circ$ bond angles.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Four domains arrange in three dimensions, pointing to corners of a tetrahedron.

examples.explanation: Electron geometry: tetrahedral; Molecular geometry: tetrahedral.
Electron geometry: tetrahedral; Molecular geometry: trigonal pyramidal ($\sim 107^\circ$).
Electron geometry: tetrahedral; Molecular geometry: bent ($\sim 104.5^\circ$).

image.caption: Two lone pairs create bent shape.

categories.description: Trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry with $90^\circ$, $120^\circ$, and $180^\circ$ angles.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Five domains arrange with 3 equatorial ($120^\circ$ apart) and 2 axial ($90^\circ$ to equatorial).

examples.explanation: Electron geometry: trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry: trigonal bipyramidal.
Electron geometry: trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry: seesaw.

categories.description: Octahedral electron geometry with $90^\circ$ bond angles.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Six domains arrange symmetrically, pointing to corners of an octahedron.

examples.explanation: Electron geometry: octahedral; Molecular geometry: octahedral.
Electron geometry: octahedral; Molecular geometry: square pyramidal.

image.caption: One lone pair creates square pyramidal shape.

examples.explanation: Electron geometry: octahedral; Molecular geometry: square planar.

image.caption: Two lone pairs opposite each other.

principles.statement: In trigonal bipyramidal geometry, lone pairs prefer equatorial positions ($120^\circ$) over axial positions ($90^\circ$) to minimize repulsion.
principles.application: In $\text{SF}_4$, the lone pair occupies an equatorial position, giving seesaw molecular geometry rather than placing the lone pair axially.
principles.analogy: Like choosing a seat at a table: equatorial positions have more space (fewer $90^\circ$ interactions) than axial positions.

sections.section_title: Bond Angle Deviations from Ideal Geometry
sections.introduction: While VSEPR predicts ideal bond angles, real molecules often show deviations due to differences in repulsion strength between lone pairs and bonding pairs, and due to differences in atom electronegativity.

key_concept.definition: Bond angles deviate from ideal values when lone pairs are present or when bonded atoms have different electronegativities, causing unequal electron distribution.
key_concept.context: Understanding deviations helps predict molecular polarity and reactivity more accurately.

characteristics: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs
Each lone pair reduces adjacent bond angles by $\sim 2-3^\circ$
Multiple lone pairs have cumulative effect
classifications.behavior: Lone pair repulsion pushes bonding pairs closer together, compressing bond angles below ideal values.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_4$: $109.5^\circ$ (0 lone pairs); $\text{NH}_3$: $107^\circ$ (1 lone pair); $\text{H}_2\text{O}$: $104.5^\circ$ (2 lone pairs).

image.alt: Bond angle trend with lone pairs
image.caption: Increasing lone pairs decrease bond angles.

classifications.value: More Electronegative Atoms Reduce Bond Angles
characteristics: Electronegative atoms pull bonding electrons away from central atom
Reduced electron density near central atom decreases repulsion
classifications.behavior: When terminal atoms are highly electronegative, bonding electron density shifts away from the central atom, reducing domain repulsion.

examples.explanation: $\text{NH}_3$: $107^\circ$; $\text{NF}_3$: $102^\circ$. Fluorine's electronegativity pulls electrons, reducing repulsion.

characteristics: Multiple bonds have higher electron density
Multiple bonds repel adjacent domains more strongly
classifications.behavior: Double and triple bonds act as larger electron domains, pushing adjacent bonds away.

examples.explanation: $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bond repels $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds; $\text{H}-\text{C}-\text{H}$ angle is $116^\circ$ (larger than ideal $120^\circ$ for other angles).

principles.statement: Lone pair-lone pair repulsion is approximately $2\times$ stronger than bonding pair-bonding pair repulsion, explaining significant angle compression.
principles.application: Water's $104.5^\circ$ angle (vs. ideal $109.5^\circ$) reflects two lone pairs exerting strong repulsion on bonding pairs.
principles.analogy: Like springs: lone pairs are stiffer springs that push harder than bonding pair springs.

sections.introduction: Molecular geometry directly determines whether a molecule is polar or nonpolar, which affects intermolecular forces, solubility, boiling point, and chemical reactivity.

key_concept.definition: A molecule is polar if it has polar bonds arranged asymmetrically, creating a net dipole moment. Symmetric arrangements of polar bonds can cancel, making the molecule nonpolar.
key_concept.context: VSEPR geometry predictions enable polarity determination before experimental measurement.

categories.description: Symmetric geometries where bond dipoles cancel completely.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Vector sum of individual bond dipoles equals zero due to symmetry.

examples.explanation: Two polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bonds point in opposite directions; dipoles cancel → nonpolar molecule.

image.caption: Opposing dipoles cancel in linear geometry.

examples.explanation: Four polar $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bonds arranged symmetrically; vector sum = 0 → nonpolar molecule.
Three polar $\text{B}-\text{F}$ bonds at $120^\circ$; dipoles cancel → nonpolar molecule.

categories.description: Asymmetric geometries where bond dipoles do not cancel, creating a net dipole moment.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Vector sum of bond dipoles is nonzero; molecule has positive and negative ends.

examples.explanation: Two polar $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bonds at $104.5^\circ$; dipoles do not cancel → polar molecule ($\mu = 1.85~\text{D}$).
Three polar $\text{N}-\text{H}$ bonds with lone pair; dipoles add → polar molecule ($\mu = 1.47~\text{D}$).
Four bonds but different atoms; $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ dipole not cancelled → polar molecule.

principles.statement: Highly symmetric molecular geometries (linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal bipyramidal, octahedral) with identical terminal atoms produce nonpolar molecules.
principles.application: $\text{SF}_6$ (octahedral, all F) is nonpolar; $\text{SF}_5\text{Cl}$ (octahedral, mixed terminals) is polar.
principles.analogy: Like a tug-of-war: if teams are evenly matched and opposite (symmetric), the rope doesn't move (nonpolar); if unbalanced, the rope moves toward one side (polar).

sections.introduction: Apply VSEPR theory to predict molecular geometry, bond angles, and polarity for various molecules.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for $\text{PCl}_3$
worked_examples.problem: Determine the electron geometry, molecular geometry, and bond angles for phosphorus trichloride, $\text{PCl}_3$.

step_by_step.explanation: P (Group 15) has 5 valence $e^-$; 3 Cl atoms contribute 3 bonding pairs. Total: 5 + 3(7) = 26 valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: P central, 3 P-Cl bonds, 1 lone pair on P

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding domains (P-Cl bonds) + 1 lone pair = 4 electron domains.
4 domains → tetrahedral electron geometry (ideal angle $109.5^\circ$).
Ignore lone pair for molecular geometry: 3 atoms + 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal.
Lone pair compresses angles below ideal $109.5^\circ$; expect $\sim 107^\circ$ (similar to $\text{NH}_3$).

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{PCl}_3$: Electron geometry = tetrahedral; Molecular geometry = trigonal pyramidal; Bond angle $\approx 107^\circ$. The molecule is polar due to asymmetric geometry.
worked_examples.concept_applied: VSEPR Theory with Lone Pair Effects

worked_examples.title: Determining Molecular Polarity: $\text{SF}_4$
worked_examples.problem: Is sulfur tetrafluoride, $\text{SF}_4$, polar or nonpolar? Explain using VSEPR theory.

step_by_step.explanation: S (Group 16) has 6 valence $e^-$; 4 F atoms form 4 bonds. Total: 6 + 4(7) = 34 valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: S central, 4 S-F bonds, 1 lone pair on S

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding domains + 1 lone pair = 5 electron domains.
5 domains → trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry.
Lone pair occupies equatorial position (minimizes $90^\circ$ repulsions); 4 atoms → seesaw molecular geometry.
Seesaw geometry is asymmetric; bond dipoles do not cancel. Lone pair contributes to asymmetry.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{SF}_4$ is polar. The seesaw molecular geometry (derived from trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry with one equatorial lone pair) is asymmetric, so the four S-F bond dipoles do not cancel, creating a net dipole moment.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Bond Angles: $\text{NO}_2^-$ vs. $\text{NO}_2^+$
worked_examples.problem: Which ion has the larger bond angle: nitrite ($\text{NO}_2^-$) or nitronium ($\text{NO}_2^+$)? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: N central, 2 bonds to O, 1 lone pair on N. Total domains = 3 (2 bonding + 1 lone).
3 domains → trigonal planar electron geometry ($120^\circ$ ideal). Lone pair compresses angle to $\sim 115^\circ$.
N central, 2 double bonds to O, 0 lone pairs on N. Total domains = 2 (2 bonding, 0 lone).
2 domains → linear electron geometry, ideal $180^\circ$ bond angle.
$180^\circ$ ($\text{NO}_2^+$) > $115^\circ$ ($\text{NO}_2^-$). Fewer domains and no lone pairs give larger angle.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{NO}_2^+$ has the larger bond angle ($180^\circ$, linear) compared to $\text{NO}_2^-$ ($\approx 115^\circ$, bent). The difference arises because $\text{NO}_2^+$ has only 2 electron domains (no lone pairs) while $\text{NO}_2^-$ has 3 domains including a lone pair that compresses the bond angle.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron Domain Count and Bond Angle Prediction

worked_examples.title: Geometry and Polarity: $\text{XeF}_4$
worked_examples.problem: Determine the molecular geometry of xenon tetrafluoride, $\text{XeF}_4$, and predict whether it is polar or nonpolar.

step_by_step.explanation: Xe (Group 18) has 8 valence $e^-$; 4 F atoms form 4 bonds. Total: 8 + 4(7) = 36 valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: Xe central, 4 Xe-F bonds, 2 lone pairs on Xe

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding domains + 2 lone pairs = 6 electron domains.
6 domains → octahedral electron geometry.
2 lone pairs occupy opposite (axial) positions to minimize repulsion; 4 F atoms in square plane → square planar.
Square planar geometry is symmetric; opposite Xe-F bond dipoles cancel. Lone pairs are opposite and cancel.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{XeF}_4$: Electron geometry = octahedral; Molecular geometry = square planar; Nonpolar. The symmetric square planar arrangement causes the four Xe-F bond dipoles to cancel, and the two opposite lone pairs also cancel, resulting in no net dipole moment.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Expanded Octet VSEPR and Symmetry-Polarity Relationship

summary: VSEPR theory predicts molecular geometry by arranging electron domains (bonding pairs and lone pairs) to minimize repulsion. Electron geometry includes all domains; molecular geometry shows only atom positions. Common geometries include linear (2 domains), trigonal planar (3), tetrahedral (4), trigonal bipyramidal (5), and octahedral (6). Lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs, compressing bond angles below ideal values. Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity and geometric symmetry: symmetric arrangements of polar bonds can cancel to produce nonpolar molecules. Understanding VSEPR enables prediction of molecular shape, bond angles, and polarity from Lewis structures.
